serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chilli lamb with green pepper is approximately 250 g, representing a single-person main-course portion as served at a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, composite cuts, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with stir-fried lamb with vegetables composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb composite (NDB 17003 / FDC ID 174352) adjusted for stir-fry preparation with green pepper, chilli, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and cooking oil. Green pepper contribution (USDA FDC 170108) and cooking oil addition (~5 g oil per 100 g dish) incorporated into the composite estimate. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish style. Values represent a cooked, ready-to-eat 100 g portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; meat and vegetable stir-fry dishes analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chilli lamb with green pepper. GI estimated at 20 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of mixed meat-and-vegetable stir-fry dishes as low-GI foods (GI range 15–30). The dish is predominantly protein and fat with a low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g), consistent with a low GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (20 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing 46% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 20, this dish has a negligible glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions without added starchy sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from lamb and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 3.6 g saturated fat per 100 g and elevated sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size, choose leaner lamb cuts, and request lower-sodium preparation to align with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Lamb with Green Pepper (青椒炒羊肉, Qīng jiāo chǎo yángròu) is a classic Chinese stir-fry dish featuring thinly sliced lamb cooked at high heat in a wok together with fresh green peppers, dried or fresh chillies, garlic, ginger, and spring onion, seasoned with soy sauce and a small amount of cooking oil. The dish is associated with the northwestern Chinese culinary tradition, particularly Xinjiang and Inner Mongolian influences, where lamb is the dominant protein. The texture is tender and slightly chewy from the quick-cooked lamb, with a crisp bite from the green pepper and a fragrant, mildly spicy flavour profile. In Singapore, this dish is served at northern Chinese restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant and is typically ordered as a shared main course alongside rice or noodles. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (~14.8 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (~10.5 g per 100 g), with a low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g) that results in a low glycemic index and glycemic load, making it broadly compatible with low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g), requiring attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally halal and dairy-free, and contains soy as a declared allergen from the soy sauce component.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb composite with a composite adjustment for stir-fry preparation, green pepper, and soy sauce seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database classification of mixed meat-and-vegetable stir-fry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (cooked lamb) has strong USDA source support and the adjustments are methodologically defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate: cooked lamb base from USDA FDC SR Legacy (FDC ID 174352), green pepper contribution from USDA FDC 170108, and cooking oil addition (~5 g per 100 g dish). Sodium is elevated to ~420 mg/100 g to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this lamb composite entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb composite and are plausible for this dish type. 4. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was located in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input food_group was listed as 'Grains and staples', which is incorrect for a lamb stir-fry dish; corrected to 'Meat and alternatives'. 6. The input cuisine_tags included 'Noodle', which is not applicable to this dish; this tag has been disregarded. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002764 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code was found for this specific preparation.
